text: Mental fatigue caused by prolonged demanding cognitive activity reduces time to exhaustion during high-intensity exercise by approximately 15%.
Mental fatigue impairs exercise tolerance through higher perception of effort rather than through cardiorespiratory or musculoenergetic mechanisms.
Mentally fatigued individuals rate perception of effort significantly higher during intense exercise despite unchanged physiological responses (heart rate, VO2, lactate, cardiac output).
As little as 90 minutes of demanding cognitive work before exercise is sufficient to induce mental fatigue that measurably impairs subsequent physical performance.
Mentally fatigued individuals reach their maximal perceived exertion and disengage from exercise earlier, even though RPE increases at the same rate over time as in a non-fatigued state.
Mental fatigue does not reduce intrinsic motivation or self-reported desire to succeed at a physical task, suggesting the performance decrement is perceptual rather than motivational.
